coredata.dc:title: Recycling of cathode materials from spent Li-ion batteries using bio-based reductant: Experimental insights and molecular dynamic simulations

coredata.dc:description: The surging volume of spent lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) from consumer electronics and electric vehicles emphasizes the need for recycling routes that are both high-yield and environmentally benign. Plant-derived reagents can rival conventional H2SO4/H2O2 systems while eliminating hazardous oxidants and valorizing agricultural residues. This study presents an eco-friendly method for hydrometallurgical dissolution of critical metals from NMC-type LIB black mass using bell pepper extract (BPE) as a bio-reduction. BPE exhibited high antioxidant activity (306.9 ± 1.1 mg Trolox/L) and total phenolic content (228.6 mg gallic acid/L), which play a crucial role in facilitating metal dissolution. Phenolic and flavonoid constituents in BPE donate electrons to reduce Ni, Mn, and Co oxides to their soluble divalent ionic forms via a bio-assisted redox mechanism in acidic media. Process variables were optimized by batch leaching in 2 M H2SO4. Under optimum conditions (30 vol% BPE, 20 g/L pulp density, 70 °C, 120 min) leaching efficiencies reached 98.7 % Li, 98.9 % Ni, 97.9 % Mn, and 96.1 % Co achieving nearly complete dissolution and yielding two–to–threefold higher efficiencies than conventional acid leaching without BPE. SEM-EDS confirmed that the post-leach residue is essentially metal-free. Density functional theory (DFT) and molecular dynamics (MD) simulations provided mechanistic insights into the interactions between H2SO4 and metal ions (Co2 +, Ni2+, Mn2+, Li+), revealing the role of surface charge density and solvation structure in leaching behavior. Radial distribution functions and coordination number analysis confirmed valence-dependent sulfate complexation and distinct hydration shells, supporting the observed trends in leaching efficiency.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The surging volume of spent lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) from consumer electronics and electric vehicles emphasizes the need for recycling routes that are both high-yield and environmentally benign. Plant-derived reagents can rival conventional H2SO4/H2O2 systems while eliminating hazardous oxidants and valorizing agricultural residues. This study presents an eco-friendly method for hydrometallurgical dissolution of critical metals from NMC-type LIB black mass using bell pepper extract (BPE) as a bio-reduction. BPE exhibited high antioxidant activity (306.9 ± 1.1 mg Trolox/L) and total phenolic content (228.6 mg gallic acid/L), which play a crucial role in facilitating metal dissolution. Phenolic and flavonoid constituents in BPE donate electrons to reduce Ni, Mn, and Co oxides to their soluble divalent ionic forms via a bio-assisted redox mechanism in acidic media. Process variables were optimized by batch leaching in 2 M H2SO4. Under optimum conditions (30 vol% BPE, 20 g/L pulp density, 70 °C, 120 min) leaching efficiencies reached 98.7 % Li, 98.9 % Ni, 97.9 % Mn, and 96.1 % Co achieving nearly complete dissolution and yielding two–to–threefold higher efficiencies than conventional acid leaching without BPE. SEM-EDS confirmed that the post-leach residue is essentially metal-free. Density functional theory (DFT) and molecular dynamics (MD) simulations provided mechanistic insights into the interactions between H2SO4 and metal ions (Co2 +, Ni2+, Mn2+, Li+), revealing the role of surface charge density and solvation structure in leaching behavior. Radial distribution functions and coordination number analysis confirmed valence-dependent sulfate complexation and distinct hydration shells, supporting the observed trends in leaching efficiency.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: With the significant increase in LIBs usage in recent years, a vast amount of waste from these batteries is projected to be produced in the near future, posing serious environmental concerns if not properly managed and recycled (Xiao et al., 2025). Additionally, LIBs are composed of substantial amounts of metals, including nickel (Ni), cobalt (Co), manganese (Mn), and lithium (Li). Notably, 35 % of the world's annual lithium production and 25 % of cobalt are utilized in LIBs manufacturing. Cobalt, in particular, is classified as a hazardous metal and a potential carcinogen (Krishnan et al., 2021; Sethurajan et al., 2022). Therefore, adopting environmentally friendly and efficient recycling methods for used batteries is crucial to guarantee the materials supply chain in battery industries (Ebrahimi et al., 2023), supporting the circular economy and mitigating environmental impacts, particularly by reducing CO2 emissions (Srinivasan, Shanthakumar and Ashok, 2025).

Most of the spent LIBs are composed of 80–85 % metal oxide powder, 10 % polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF), and 5 % black acetylene. Cathode made from materials such as LiCoO2, LiFePO4, LiMn2O4, and LiNi1/3Mn1/3Co1/3O2, and anode typically composed of graphite (Kim et al., 2021). The composition of LIBs varies slightly among manufacturers but typically includes 5–20 % Co, 5–10 % Ni, 5–7 % Li, 15 % organic compounds, and 7 % plastics (Jung et al., 2021; Liu et al., 2022). Aluminum is also present as the current collector foil coated with active electrode materials (Tembo et al., 2025). Among these metals, cobalt is the most expensive and requires a reducing environment for effective hydrometallurgical recovery. This approach also improves the leaching efficiency of nickel and manganese. Therefore, the leaching of NMC-type black mass is most effective in the presence of a reducing agent (Lie and Liu, 2021; Meng, Zhang and Dong, 2017).

Most spent batteries are recycled using a hydrometallurgical method, where cathode materials are dissolved in inorganic/organic acids with the help of reducing agents, followed by metal separation techniques such as precipitation, electrochemical, or solvent extraction (Khodadadmahmoudi et al., 2023). Among various reductants explored, hydrogen peroxide (H₂O₂) is the most widely used due to its significant impact on leaching efficiency. For instance, Nayl et al. reported that adding 4 % H₂O₂ to a 2 M sulfuric acid solution increased Co dissolution rate from 42.7 % to 99.6 %, Ni from 82.2 % to 99.4 %, and Mn from 51.6 % to 97.8 % (Nayl et al., 2014). Although H₂O₂ remains the most widely used reductant, it decomposes rapidly in acidic and metal-rich leach solutions. As a result, excess amounts must be added to maintain the desired redox potential, which consequently increases operating costs.

Several alternative reducing agents to hydrogen peroxide have been proposed to improve metal dissolution efficiency from spent LIBs, as indicated in Table 1. For example, metallic iron scrap has been shown to act as an effective reductant in sulfuric acid, increasing Co dissolution rate from 43.2 % to over 99 % by generating Fe²⁺ in situ (Ghassa et al., 2021). Hydrazine sulfate (N₂H₆SO₄), when added at 30 g/L, resulted in dissolution efficiency rates of 95 % for Co, 96 % for Ni, and 86 % for Mn (Yang et al., 2020). Another study compared several reducing agents such as Na₂S₂O₅, Na₂SO₃, H₂O₂, and iron scrap, among those Na₂S₂O₅ achieved the highest leaching rate, even outperforming hydrogen peroxide (Vieceli et al., 2018). Additionally, in an ammonia-based leaching system, 0.5 M ammonium sulfite ((NH₄)₂SO₃) significantly increased Co and Ni leaching efficiency from near 0–78 % and 26 %, respectively (Ku et al., 2016). Furthermore, several studies investigated the use of organic compounds such as glucose and deep eutectic solvents as a reductant in the leaching of BM (Chen et al., 2016, 2023; Meshram et al., 2016). Additionally, interest is shifting to biomass-derived reductants, which offer comparable redox power while being safer, renewable, and sourced from low-cost waste streams. Zhang et al. showed that grape seed could act as an efficient reductant in 1.5 M acid malic media and increase Li and Co dissolution efficiency from 66 % to 99 % and 44–92 %, respectively (Zhang et al., 2018). The use of Phytolacca americana (PA) and tea waste (TW) as green reductants for leaching of metals from LIBs was studied by Chen et al. They found that the dissolution with TW in a citric acid environment was significantly higher than with PA. Notably, the leaching efficiency achieved with TW was comparable to that of using hydrogen peroxide as a reductant (Chen et al., 2015).

Although using biological reducing reagents can save the environment and decrease process costs, only a few studies have been carried out to find a biodegradable reductant for LIBs leaching. Several agricultural and farming wastes were initially screened as potential bioreductants based on their antioxidant content and metal reduction performance. Bell Pepper Extract (BPE) stands out among plant-based reductants because it packs more reducing equivalents per liter of leach liquor than most agricultural by-products that have been tried so far. BPE contains bioactive compounds like flavone, flavonol, xanthine, cellulose, and reducing sugars, which can act as a reductant in the aqueous environment (Eslami, Jafarizadeh-Malmiri and Khonakdar, 2022; Karimi et al., 2019). Also, low lignin content of BPE also maintained a pulp viscosity, ensuring good processability compared with other bio-derived slurries that tend to thicken rapidly. The use of pepper extract as a reductant for synthesizing nanoparticles to remove colored pollutants from water has been previously reported (Khaghani et al., 2017), and also for synthesizing silver nanoparticles (Vatandost et al., 2016). However, its potential as a reductant for metal dissolution and recovery in battery recycling has not yet been explored.

Molecular dynamics is a valuable tool that can be utilized in the dissolution processes of metal-containing materials to determine mechanisms and provide valuable information. Recent studies on the dissolution of LIBs cathodes in a deep eutectic solvent (DES) composed of choline chloride and glucose have demonstrated that Co, Ni, Mn, and Li exhibit a strong tendency to form complexes with the chloride present in this DES (Behnajady et al., 2024). Meanwhile, molecular dynamics computations have shown the minimal impact of oxygen and hydrogen in this mixture through radial distribution function (RDF) calculations. Therefore, using these data can serve as a powerful tool for determining reaction mechanisms in hydrometallurgy and solvent metallurgy. Building on this understanding, the present study aims to develop an efficient, economical, and environmentally sustainable recycling route for NMC-type spent LIBs using a plant-based bio-reductant. Specifically, bell-pepper extract was investigated as a novel and green reducing agent for the leaching of Li, Co, Mn, and Ni in a sulfuric acid medium. The effects of key operational parameters including acid concentration, BPE dosage, solid content, temperature, and leaching time were systematically evaluated, and the underlying redox mechanism was supported by molecular dynamics simulations and experimental characterization.

In this study, the challenges of recycling lithium-ion batteries at pilot and industrial scales are examined, particularly considering the variation in battery types and brands. A total of fifty spent smartphone pouch cells from four different brands were collected for the experiments. The cells were the flat pouch-type with polymer-laminated pouches with dimensions 4–6 mm × 38–51 mm × 72–92 mm and a mean mass of 40 g.

To avoid combustion, all cells were discharged before dismantling and then crushed with a shredder. The particles were subsequently ground using a cutting mill equipped with a 2 mm screen. Aluminum, copper, and plastic parts remained in the coarse fraction, while cathode particles accumulated in the finer part, which was called black mass. A 75 μm screen was used to separate the black mass from coarse materials, as shown in Fig. 1 (Ahuis et al., 2024; Khodadadmahmoudi et al., 2023). It should be noted that the term “black mass” in this study refers to the cathode material.

Inductively coupled plasma mass spectroscopy (ICP-MS, PerkinElmer-optima8000, USA) was employed to determine the metal content in the black mass. For ICP-OES analysis, 0.1 g of the processed cathode (black mass) was digested in aqua regia (a mixture of hydrochloric acid and nitric acid from Merck in a volume ratio of 3:1). The resulting solution was analyzed, and the average of three replicate measurements is reported in Table 3.

The feed was characterized for mineralogy by X-ray diffraction (Philips-Xpert Pro, the Netherlands) with Cu Kα radiation at a voltage of 40 kV and 30 mA, in the 2θ range of 10–70° and X-ray fluorescence (Philips PW1820, the Netherlands). The morphology and elemental analysis of the feed and the leaching residues were determined by scanning electron microscopy (LEO1450 UP, Zeiss, Germany) and the energy dispersive spectrometer (EDS). In this analysis, samples were coated with a ∼30 nm layer of gold before imaging tests.

Fresh bell peppers (Capsicum annuum) were purchased from a local market. The samples were thoroughly rinsed with tap and deionized water, air-dried, and pulverized. To prepare the bell pepper extract (BPE), 4 g of the fine powder was mixed with 100 mL of deionized water and stirred at 200 rpm for 60 min at 70 °C using a magnetic hotplate stirrer (HSD330, MTops, Korea). The slurry was cooled down to room temperature, filtered through a filter paper, and stored for the leaching process. To prevent structural changes, the BPE was freshly prepared before each leaching experiment. BPE was characterized by Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) and Ultraviolet-visible (UV-Vis) spectrophotometry. Note that the total antioxidant capacity (DPPH) and total phenol content (FCR) of BPE were measured based on the previous methods and studies (Baliyan et al., 2022; Rahman et al., 2015). It should be noted that the sulfuric acid (H2SO4, 98 %) with analytical grade was purchased from Merck, Germany.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experimental procedure for the leaching process
ce:section.ce:para: Leaching experiments were carried out on the black mass extracted from LIBs. This study investigated the effects of five different parameters, including BPE concentration, solid/liquid ratio, leaching time, sulfuric acid concentration, and temperature, on the leaching of Li, Ni, Mn and Co using the one factor at a time (OFAT) methodology. In this approach, each parameter is optimized individually while all other parameters are constant (Javanshir et al., 2024; Zhang et al., 2018). The leaching experiments and all parameter levels are listed in Table 2.

The dissolution tests were conducted in a 200 mL Round-bottom flask with a certain BPE concentration and acid ratio (% vol.). A magnetic hot plate (model HSD 330, MTOPS, Korea) was used for conducting experiments. The leaching reactor was equipped with a glass condenser to avoid the leaching regent evaporation and volume changes. All the teaching tests were carried out at 300 rpm. Finally, the filtrate was collected, and the residue was dried at 40 °C in a drying oven overnight. The pregnant leach solutions (PLS) were analyzed by ICP-OES to determine the Li, Co, Mn, and Ni content. The leaching efficiency was calculated using Eq. (1).
(1)
Leaching efficiency ( % ) =
C × V
M × wt . %
× 100

Where C (%) is the concentration of metals in the leaching solution (PLS), wt% is the feed grade of the black mass, V (mL) is the volume of the leaching solution at the end of leaching, and M (g) refers to the mass of the solid sample used in each experiment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Molecular dynamics (MD) and density functional theory (DFT) simulation
ce:section.ce:para: The interaction of H2SO4 with various cations (Li+, Co2+, Ni2+, and Mn2+) was examined by analyzing their radial distribution functions (RDFs). All simulations were conducted using the Universal force field within the Material Studio software (Version 2020). The generation of molecular configurations and geometry optimization of SO4 2- and H2O were performed using the Materials Studio software (Jafari et al., 2025). Energy minimization was performed using the Forcite module, where the Smart algorithm was applied for geometry optimization. The convergence thresholds were defined as 0.001 kcal/mol for energy and 0.5 kcal/mol/Å for force. The Universal force field was utilized for energy calculations, maintaining the original charges based on previous studies (Behnajady et al., 2024; Shahrezaei et al., 2024). Electrostatic and van der Waals interactions were analyzed atomically using a cubic spline truncation method, with specified cutoff distances, spline widths, and buffer widths for each type of interaction (Behnajady et al., 2024). Based on the optimum condition for leaching of black mass, it was estimated that the reaction medium contained 55.3 M of H2O and 2.0 M of sulfate ions. To ensure accurate proportions within the simulation box in Material Studio, a large number of H2O molecules were needed, specifically 55.3 molecules per nickel ion. However, this high ratio significantly increased the system size and, thereby, the simulation takes a long time. To improve model accuracy while reducing the system size, it was assumed that 830 H2O molecules, approximately 55.3 multiplied by 15, represented 30 sulfate ions. Next, an amorphous simulation cell was then built using Amorphous Cell Tools, capable of accommodating 830 molecules of H2O and 30 SO4 2-, along with 24 H+, 10 Li+, 8 Co2+, 4 Mn2+, and 1 Ni2+ ions.

The generated amorphous cell underwent geometry optimization to remove unfavorable interactions and reduce the overall energy of the system. Geometry optimization was performed using the Smart algorithm, with convergence limits defined as 2 × 10−5 kcal/mol, 0.001 kcal/mol/A, and 1 × 10−5 Å for energy, force, and displacement, respectively. In the subsequent step, the ensemble maintaining fixed particle count, volume, and temperature (NVT) and the ensemble with a fixed number of particles, constant pressure, and temperature (NPT) were employed to achieve conditions of 343.1 K and 1 atm (Yousefi Seyf and Haghtalab, 2016). The NVT ensemble was split into two segments: an initial pre-NVT phase running for 500 ps, followed by an NVT phase extending for 10,000 ps, where the Andersen method and velocity scale were used as the thermostat and as the thermostat, respectively. Once the cell achieved 343.1 °C, the NPT ensemble was applied to stabilize the pressure at 1 atm. Additional information has been comprehensively described in an earlier research (Behnajady et al., 2024).

The radial distribution function (RDF) was utilized to analyze the interactions among various cations (Li+, Co2+, Ni2+, and Mn2+) and anions by measuring the distances between their central atoms. The RDF, denoted as g(rij), represents the probability of locating a molecule of type "i" near a molecule of type "j" at a specific radial distance (rij). This function is particularly useful for illustrating the interaction strength between solute molecules and their surrounding solvent molecules, which is closely related to cation complexes. Using MD simulation data, the RDFs for Li+, Co2+, Ni2+, and Mn2+ were computed. The equation of the RDF is given by the following equation (Yu et al., 2024):
(2)

In this context, dr, r, ρ, and T represent the incremental distance difference, the distance from the reference molecule, the average density of the molecular system, and the total simulation time, respectively. ΔN and N correspond to the number of molecules between r and r + dr volume, and the total number of molecules, respectively.

Additionally, the DMol3 module was employed to compute the sigma profile and the electron charge density of H2SO4 using the conductor-like screening model (COSMO) method (Behnajady et al., 2024; Mullins et al., 2008). This probability distribution is derived by averaging the surface charge densities obtained from a COSMO calculation.

Here, Ai denotes the total cavity surface area, ni(σ) represents the count of segments having a discretized surface charge density σ, and Ai(σ) is the combined surface area of all segments with a specific charge density σ.

Table 3 shows elemental analyses of the black mass. According to the result, the key elements in this sample are Co, Mn, Li, and Ni, with concentrations of 24.8 %, 10.3 %, 3.6 %, and 2.9 %, respectively. It is worth noting that the low concentrations of iron and aluminum in the sample are advantageous, as they do not adversely affect the leaching process or the separation of the main elements. Previous studies have indicated that high iron and aluminum content in black mass can hinder the dissolution of key elements and even pose challenges during the separation process (Chernyaev et al., 2023; Punt et al., 2022).

The XRD pattern of the black mass is shown in Fig. 2. XRD analysis confirmed that the primary crystalline phases present in the black mass were lithium cobalt oxide (LiCoO2, JCPDS 00–016–0427), lithium nickel manganese oxide (LiNi0.18Mn1.82O4, JCPDS 01–089–0107), lithium manganese oxide (Li2MnO3, JCPDS 01–073–0152), and graphite (C, JCPDS 00–026–1080). These compounds represent the main active materials commonly found in end-of-life lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) (Behnajady et al., 2024; Dobó et al., 2023). The diffraction peaks corresponding to crystalline graphite further confirm the presence of residual anode material.

The particle size distribution was determined using a laser-size particle analyzer (Fritsch, ANALYSETTE 22, Germany). As shown in Fig. 3, 90 % of the black mass particles are smaller than 37 μm, indicating a d90 value of 37 μm.

Yellow bell pepper (Capsicum annuum) is rich in vitamins, especially vitamin C, and flavonoid compounds (Nerdy, 2018). It contains abundant flavonoids, carotenoids, phenolic acids, and plant sterols, which serve as precursors to vitamin D. These properties make it highly valuable in the food industry, where it is utilized for its vibrant pigments and distinctive aroma (Lobo et al., 2018).

FTIR was conducted to identify the biomolecules present in the extract responsible for the reduction process ( Fig. 4). The spectra revealed the presence of various functional groups. The wide peak at 3438 cm−1 is related to the stretching vibrations of the O-H groups, indicating the presence of the hydroxyl group coming from water and phenolic compounds (Kaur and Kaur, 2020; Singh et al., 2016). The absorption peak at 1100 cm−1 is attributed to (CH-CH) and C-O stretching, characteristic of phenols (Kaur and Kaur, 2020). It is also reported that this strong stretching vibration can be related to different saccharide groups, such as glucose, fructose, and sucrose (Rybak et al., 2022). The strong band at 1638 cm−1 is associated with the stretching vibrations of the CO groups of the amide I band (Shirmehenji et al., 2021). Additionally, the stretching vibration at 646 cm−1 confirmed the presence of the R–CH group (Yuan et al., 2017). Conclusively, the FT-IR spectrum of BPE verified the presence of ascorbic acid and phenolic compounds, which play a critical role in the bio-reduction of metal ions in waste LIBs.

Bell peppers are rich in ascorbic acid, which is commonly analyzed using UV–VIS spectroscopy due to its absorption of UV radiation in the 200–400 nm range (Afnani et al.,2023; Al Majidi and Al Qubury, 2016). Fig. 5 displays the UV-Vis spectra of the aqueous solution prepared as BPE, measured within the range of 190–900 nm. Various studies have shown the absorption bands at around 400–450 nm in the UV–visible spectra. For example, it is reported that the λmax measurement of a vitamin C solution indicates a maximum wavelength of 373.5 nm (Afnani et al., 2023; Rödiger et al., 2021).

The antioxidant activity and total phenolic content of BPE were evaluated using two methods. The first method employed was the 2,2-Diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl (DPPH) assay, a widely used, quick, simple, and cost-effective technique for measuring antioxidant properties. This method utilizes free radicals to assess the ability of substances to act as hydrogen donors or free radical scavengers (FRS) (Baliyan et al., 2022; Rahman et al., 2015). The antioxidant activity of BPE was determined to be approximately 306.9 ± 1.1 mg Trolox/L. Secondly, the Folin Ciocalteu Reagent (FCR) method, also known as the gallic acid equivalence (GAE) technique, is a colorimetric in vitro assay used to measure phenolic and polyphenolic antioxidants. This method employs phosphotungstate and phosphomolybdate reagents. The FCR value of BPE was determined to be approximately 228.6 mg gallic acid/L (Baliyan et al., 2022). Together, these analyses (high DPPH radical scavenging capacity and total phenolic content) verify that BPE contains functional groups (e.g., –OH, CH–CH, and phenolic structures) capable of acting as effective bio-reductants during metal extraction from spent LIBs.

Leaching efficiency can be greatly improved through the addition of a reductant. Most researchers also suggested H2O2 as a reducing agent because the oxygen produced by its decomposition in the leaching process converts Co(III) to Co(II) and aids in dissolution. In this study, in order to examine the dissolution behavior of metals from the black mass, BPE was used as a reductant during the leaching reaction in the presence and absence of sulfuric acid as a main reagent and compared with H2O2, as shown in Fig. 6.

In the first experiment, sulfuric acid was used without the addition of a reducing agent. The leaching conditions were set with 2 M sulfuric acid at 70 °C, a solid-to-liquid ratio of 5 g/L, and a leaching period of 120 min. Under these conditions, the leaching efficiencies were relatively low, achieving approximately 80.4 % for Li, 49.7 % for Ni, 78.4 % for Co, and 35 % for Mn. The leaching of metals was also evaluated with the addition of 5 % H2O2 as a reductant, based on previously reported conditions. It was observed that the leaching efficiency increased compared to the case without a reductant. According to Fig. 6, all metals completely dissolved when 30 % (v/v) BPE was added to the leaching medium. However, it is noteworthy that the effect of H2O2 was not as significant as that of BPE. In the fourth test, BPE was used as the primary reagent, without sulfuric acid, to dissolve metals from the black mass. As expected, the metal dissolution percentages were very low, with all metals dissolving at rates below 20 %. In conclusion, BPE acts as a strong reducing agent, significantly enhancing the rate of metal dissolution.

The impact of the biodegradable reducing agent BPE, in concentrations ranging from 0 % to 50 % (v/v), on the leaching of valuable metals from black mass was investigated as an initial parameter. Fig. 7a illustrates the effect of BPE concentration on leaching efficiency using 2 M sulfuric acid at a temperature of 70 ºC, with a solid/liquid ratio of 5 g/L over a 30-minute leaching period. The experimental results reveal a clear correlation between the concentration of BPE and leaching efficiency. Increasing the BPE concentration to 30 % (v/v) led to a gradual rise in Li extraction from 80.4 % to 98.6 %. Additionally, it was observed that the dissolution of Co and Ni increased significantly by 20 % when the BPE concentration reached 30 % (v/v). Notably, the concentration of BPE had the most significant impact on Mn dissolution, with leaching efficiency increasing from 28.8 % to a peak of 90.4 %, almost 60 % rise, after 30 min of leaching using a 30 % (v/v) BPE concentration. However, further increasing the BPE concentration to 50 % (v/v) negatively impacted the extraction of all metals. This decline is probably due to a rise in slurry viscosity, which impedes bulk mixing and limits the solid–liquid interfacial area available for mass transfer. Excessive reducing conditions can also lead to the stabilization of metals in their reduced, less soluble forms, thereby decreasing leaching efficiency (Li et al., 2020). Based on these findings, a BPE concentration of 30 % (v/v) was selected as the optimal value for subsequent experiments.

The influence of BPE concentration was also studied over extended leaching periods, up to 120 min, as depicted in Fig. 7b. Notably, even with just 10 % (v/v) BPE, significant leaching efficiencies were achieved within 120 min: 99 % for Ni, 99 % for Mn, and 100 % for Li, with Co dissolution reaching 76.8 %. Increasing the BPE concentration to 30 % (v/v) resulted in the complete dissolution of Co, with all elements achieving 100 % leaching efficiency under these conditions.

Although individual compounds were not separated chromatographically, the redox-active fraction of BPE can be parsed into two groups already confirmed by FT-IR/UV–Vis, (1) L-ascorbic acid (E° ≈ +0.39 V vs. SHE at pH 0), and (2) O-diphenolic polyphenols (E° ≈ +0.45 V). During leaching with H₂SO₄, the reductive dissolution of metals proceeds through two parallel electron-donor pathways supplied by the bell-pepper extract (BPE):
1.
Ascorbate pathway:
(4) C₆H₈O₆ → C₆H₆O₆ + 2 H⁺ + 2 e⁻
Here, ascorbic acid is oxidized to de-hydro-ascorbic acid (Ding et al., 2025), liberating two electrons that reduce transition-metal centers in the solid:
(5) Co3+ (s) + e− → Co2+ (aq)
2.
Polyphenol (catechol) pathway:
(6) Ar (OH)₂ → ArO2 + 2H⁺ + 2 e⁻

In this reaction, a catechol unit (Ar = aryl ring from BPE phenolics) is oxidized to its o-quinone and forms a chelate with the freshly generated Me²⁺ ion, which stabilizes it in solution until sulfate ions (SO₄²⁻) displace the organic ligand (Wang et al., 2024). Together, these two half-reactions supply an excess of reducing equivalents, driving the rapid, near-quantitative dissolution of Li, Ni, Co, and Mn observed under the optimized leaching conditions.

A series of experiments was conducted to examine the influence of acidity on the leaching process while other conditions were kept constant (70 °C, 350 rpm, 5 g/L, 30 % (v/v) BPE, 30 min). Sulfuric acid was used at three distinct concentrations: 1, 2, and 3 M, along with a control test without any acid ( Fig. 8). As illustrated in Fig. 8, sulfuric acid significantly influenced the leaching of metals. At a sulfuric acid concentration of 1 M H2SO4, 98.2 % of Li, 90.7 % of Mn, 74.4 % of Ni, and 56.2 % of Co were effectively transferred into the liquid phase within a 30-minutes. These findings indicate a strong correlation between metal extraction and the concentration of hydrogen ions (Kordloo et al., 2025). It is worth noting that the dissolution rates for other metals followed the order: Li > Mn > Ni > Co. Increasing the acid concentration to 2 M resulted in a slight improvement in leaching efficiency; however, further increases beyond this point didn't make much of a difference in getting the metals out. Therefore, based on economic and environmental considerations, 2 M is the optimal acid concentration for the leaching process.

This behavior can be explained by the following chemical equations (Eq. (7) to Eq. (10)) for the dissolution of alkali metals from spent LIBs (such as LiCoO2 and LiMnO2 types) in the H2SO4 solution, both in the absence and presence of H2O2 solution (Nan et al., 2005; Zhu et al., 2012). These equations demonstrate that adding the reacting substances can facilitate the forward reaction, leading to increased leaching rates of the investigated metals. In other words, during leaching, Mn, Co, Ni, and Li undergo oxidation and reduction processes, and reductants like hydrogen peroxide accelerate the reaction.
(7) 2LiCoO2+ 3H2SO4 + 3H2O2 → 2CoSO4+Li2SO4+ 6H2O + 2O2
(8) 4LiMnO2+ 6H2SO4 → 4MnSO4 + 2Li2SO4+ 6H2O +O2
(9) 2LiMnO2+ 3H2SO4+H2O2→ 2MnSO4+Li2SO4+ 4H2O+O2
(10) 6LiNi0.33Co0.33Mn0.33O2+ 9H2SO4+ 9H2O2→3Li2SO4+ 2NiSO4 + 2CoSO4+ 2MnSO4+ 18H2O+ 6O2

The effect of leaching reaction time over a range of 0–120 min on leaching efficiency has been studied by keeping the temperature at 70 °C, the S/L ratio at 5 g/L, using 2 M H2SO4, and 30 % (v/v) BPE. The findings indicated that variations in metal dissolution could be divided into three distinct stages ( Fig. 9). In the initial stage, lasting up to 15 min, the leaching percentage showed a rapid increase, reaching 45 % for Co, 54 % for Ni, 71 % for Mn, and 89 % for Li. During the second stage, from 15 to 30 min, the leaching efficiency remained relatively stable, with minimal changes observed. In the final stage, from 30 to 120 min, dissolution increased. The leaching rate varied among the different metals, following the order of Co > Ni > Mn > Li, with maximum leaching efficiency reaching 100 % for all metals except Mn.

This behavior can be attributed to the high concentration of reagents at the beginning, which were more readily available to react with the surface of the particles, facilitating metal dissolution from the black mass. During the 15–30-minute period, diffusion into the unreacted parts of the particles likely occurred, leading to a leveling off in leaching efficiency. The significant increase in leaching during the third stage is likely due to the dissolution of valuable metals from the inner parts of the particles.

Pulp density, often known as the solid/liquid ratio, plays a crucial role in the leaching process. In this section, different amounts of black mass were added to the leaching solution, while all other conditions remained at their optimum levels and remained constant. Furthermore, samples were taken from the leachate at specific intervals (30, 60, and 120 min) for analysis. The results are depicted in Fig. 10.

Fig. 10a illustrates that Li dissolution increased by approximately 6 % when the S/L ratio was elevated from 5 g/L to 100 g/L within 30 min of leaching, reaching a dissolution rate of 94.4 %. However, the dissolution behavior of Ni, Mn, and Co varied at the same time. When the S/L ratio was increased to 20 g/L, the dissolution of these elements improved significantly; for example, Ni dissolution reached from 55.4 % to 94.7 %. However, further increases in the solid content to 60 g/L and even 100 g/L resulted in decreased leaching efficiency for these elements (Fig. 10b, c, and d).

Notably, extending the leaching time to 120 min and increasing the solid concentration to 100 g/L led to 100 % dissolution of both Li and Ni. However, it is important to highlight that this level of leaching efficiency was also achieved at a lower solid content and in a shorter time for these elements. Specifically, Ni and Co were completely transferred into the liquid phase within 60 min of leaching at an S/L ratio of 20 g/L (Fig. 10a and b).

Interestingly, extending the leaching time to 120 min and increasing the solids concentration to 100 g/L did not ensure complete dissolution of Co and Mn. In fact, increasing these parameters did not show a linear relationship with leaching rates. The highest leaching efficiency for Mn and Co were observed at 20 g/L after 120 min and at 5 g/L after 120 min, respectively (Fig. 10c and d). Therefore, to achieve the maximum dissolution of all these metals, a pulp density of 20 g/L and a leaching time of 120 min are recommended as the optimal conditions.

The leaching of black mass was studied at four different temperatures (30, 50, 70, and 90 °C), while other conditions were set at their optimum values. The results show that increasing temperature has a direct and linear relationship with the dissolution of metals from black mass ( Fig. 11).

The findings indicated that variations in metal dissolution could be divided into three distinct stages. In the initial stage, from 30 to 50 °C, the temperature had a minor effect on Co leaching, resulting in just a 10 % increase, while it significantly enhanced Mn leaching, with more than a 40 % increase observed. Notably, in the same stage, at 50 °C, only Li was completely dissolved. In the second stage, increasing the temperature to 70 °C led to the dissolution of nearly all metals except Co, resulting in a 10 % increase in Mn leaching, a 30 % increase in Ni leaching, and a 50 % increase in Co leaching. The dissolution of Co was found to be highly influenced by temperature, reaching 96 % at 70 °C; however, at an elevated temperature of 90 °C in the third stage, 100 % of Co was transferred into the aqueous phase, while the dissolution of other metals remained unaffected.

Having confirmed 70 °C as the optimum at the pulp density of 20 g/L, we next explored a more industrially relevant loading of 100 g/L both to reduce reactor volume and to verify that BPE can still drive near-quantitative metal dissolution under demanding, high-solids conditions. Therefore, a series of supplementary experiments was carried out to study the effect of temperature on leaching rates at higher pulp densities. Fig. 12 presents a comparative analysis of leaching rates at two distinct solid/liquid ratios, 20 g/L and 100 g/L, at two temperatures, 70 and 90 °C. By increasing the solid content in the leaching solution to 100 g/L, Li and Ni were completely dissolved, similar to the dissolution observed at 20 g/L. However, Mn and Co exhibited a 10 % and 20 % reduction in their leaching efficiency, respectively (Fig. 12a). However, when the temperature was elevated to 90 °C with a solid/liquid ratio of 100 g/L, complete leaching of both Co and Mn was also achieved. In other words, when the temperature reached 90 °C, all metals dissolved regardless of the increase in solid content (Fig. 12b).

Finally, the chemical analysis of leaching residue under optimal conditions using ICP-OES, as shown in Table 4, confirmed that 96.1 % of Co, 98.7 % of Li, 97.9 % of Mn, and 98.9 % of Ni were dissolved under optimized conditions, which is in agreement with previous results (Chen et al., 2015; Zhang et al., 2018).

Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) were utilized to analyze the samples before and after leaching (residue). Fig. 13a shows a black mass particle with a smooth surface, which, according to EDS analysis, exhibits sharp peaks of Ni, Mn, and Co. These findings are consistent with the chemical analysis presented in Table 3. In contrast, Fig. 13b illustrates black particles with corroded surfaces after leaching. It is noteworthy that no amount of Ni, Mn, or Co was detected in the EDS analysis of the sample after leaching, which aligns perfectly with the data in Table 4. This indicates that all Mn, Co, and Ni present in the black mass particles were completely dissolved during the leaching process.

Surface charge density describes the distribution of electron charge density near a molecule's surface, leading to attractive forces between surfaces with opposite charges (Jana et al., 2022). Electrostatic forces cause opposite charges to attract, driven by potential energy. Surface charge density and sigma profiles, as described by Mullins et al. (2006), explain this interaction. The surface charge density of H2SO4, as shown in Fig. 14a, indicates that the oxygen atoms in both the hydroxyl (OH) group and the sulfur-oxygen (SO) bond significantly influence the interactions with metal complexes of Co2+, Ni2+, Mn2+ and Li+. However, this aspect will undergo a more thorough examination in subsequent discussions to enhance precision. Fig. 14b illustrates the Sigma profile of H2SO4, indicating that the formation of H2SO4 involves bonding between electron-poor and electron-rich regions. This analysis highlights that strong hydrogen bonds in H2SO4 arise from the interactions between these differing electronic characteristics.

Our MD setup for 2.0 M H2SO4 is thermodynamically stable: energies plateau, density oscillates narrowly around ∼1.52 g cm−3, and temperature remains near ∼343 K, consistent with well-equilibrated NPT dynamics. Together, these checks support the reliability of subsequent ion–acid interaction and speciation analyses (Fig. S1).

After completing the MD computation, the relationship of interatomic distances between the different atoms in cations and solvent was analyzed using RDF analysis. Fig. 15 illustrates the RDF for Co2+, Li+, Mn2+ and Ni2+ ions in relation to (a) ꟷO in SO4 2-, (b) OꟷSꟷO in SO4 2-, and (c) H2O molecules. Each peak in the graph typically represents the abundance of neighboring atoms at distances measured from the center of one atom or molecule to the center of another. In simpler terms, a higher peak intensity indicates a statistically greater likelihood of forming that particular bond or complex. Furthermore, the graphs provide additional information about the distances between atoms, which, in the context of bonds or complexes, are typically measured in angstroms.

The peaks in the RDFs shown in Fig. 15a indicate a strong attractive interaction between the Co2+, Li+, Mn2+, and Ni2+ and ꟷO in SO4 2- at around 2.6, 2.3, 2.6, 2.4 Å, respectively. The maximum intensity for the first peaks is 2.0 for Co2+, 2.2 for Li+, 2.2 for Mn2+, and 2.5 for Co2+, indicating a low difference between cations. The slight differences in peak intensities can be explained by the higher charge density of atoms in more intense electrostatic attractions between oppositely charged ions. The second and third peaks, with significantly lower intensities at distances of 4.7 and 7.3 Å, have been observed for all cations and are referred to as second- and third-layer neighbors. It should also be noted that, statistically, they exhibit weaker intensities compared to the first-layer neighbors. This layered solvation structure is consistent with previous MD and ab initio molecular dynamics (AIMD) studies of sulfuric acid and sulfate solutions, which report well-defined ion pairing behavior in acidic media (Ansari et al., 2024; Hammerich and Buch, 2012).

From the RDF results of the metal ions and O–S–O shown in Fig. 15b, a sharp peak around 2.5 Å with an intensity of 1.5–1.6 has been observed. Meanwhile, no distinct peaks have formed at greater distances for the complexes between the Co2+, Li+, Mn2+, and Ni2+ ions and O–S–O. Therefore, it is statistically evident that this type of bond has a lower likelihood compared to the single oxygen bond (–O) with metal ions, and there will be less tendency to form bonds in subsequent layers as well. Such findings align with structural insights from AIMD simulations that highlight the preference of metal ions to coordinate with sulfate oxygen atoms rather than the entire O–S–O group (Hammerich and Buch, 2012).

One significant phenomenon in the leaching process within aqueous environments is the overlap of cations by water molecules. The RDF results for metal ions and water molecules are illustrated in Fig. 15c. The findings indicate that transition metals (Co2+, Mn2+, and Ni2+) yield nearly identical results, with the first peak appearing at a distance of 2.5 Å, followed by the second and third peaks at 5.7 and 8.2 Å, respectively. In contrast, the RDF for Li and water (H2O) reveals a sharp first peak at 3.225 Å and a second peak at 15.7 Å. This discrepancy is likely attributed to the differences in electronegativity among the elements; lithium's lower electronegativity compared to other transition metals results in water molecules being situated at greater distances from Li.

The coordination numbers of Co2+, Li+, Mn2+, and Ni2+ with various functional groups of 2.0 M H2SO4 leaching medium, namely the SO2 and O atoms of SO4 2–, as well as water molecules, offer mechanistic insights into their behavior during the leaching of black mass. The data reveal that the coordination number of Li+ with the SO2 moiety is approximately 0.48, which, when interpreted in terms of ionic valence, suggests that two Li+ ions coordinate with one SO2 group, consistent with the monovalent nature of lithium. Similarly, Co2+, Mn2+, and Ni2+, all divalent ions, show SO2 coordination numbers close to 0.8, indicating that each SO2 unit can interact with roughly one divalent ion. This proportionality implies that the coordination with SO2 is valence-dependent, reflecting the ionic charge and the number of favorable electrostatic interactions. Coordination numbers with the terminal –O atoms of SO4 2– are also consistent across all cations (ranging from ∼2.1–2.2), supporting their tendency to form stable outer-sphere ion pairs with the sulfate anion, a trend commonly reported in molecular dynamics and ab initio studies of divalent–sulfate complexes (Demol et al., 2019; Rahman et al., 2015).

A notable difference is observed in water coordination. While transition metals (Co2+, Mn2+, Ni2+) exhibit coordination numbers close to 6, consistent with classical hexaaqua complexation (Martell and Hancock, 2013; Sham, Hastings and Perlman, 1980), Li⁺ shows a higher hydration number (∼7.7), reflecting its small ionic radius and high charge density, which facilitate the formation of a more diffuse and extensive hydration shell (Marcus, 2015; Tuckerman et al., 1996). Despite this stronger hydration, Li+ still demonstrates a high leaching efficiency (98.7 %), comparable to or exceeding that of transition metals (Co: 96.1 %, Mn: 97.9 %, Ni: 98.9 %). This suggests that high hydration does not significantly hinder Li+ mobility or availability for sulfate complexation under acidic conditions. Rather, it may point to a synergistic effect in which the strong solvation enhances its diffusivity and transport toward the leaching interface.

Overall, the RDF analysis reveals key interactions between metal ions (Co2+, Mn2+, Ni2+, and Li+) and their surrounding molecules in the leaching process. Transition metals (Co2+, Mn2+, Ni2+) show strong coordination with the oxygen atoms in SO4 2–, particularly at around 2.6–2.5 Å, indicating significant electrostatic attraction, while their interaction with the entire O–S–O group is weaker, suggesting a preference for bonding with sulfate oxygen. In contrast, Li+, with its higher hydration number, forms a more extensive hydration shell but still exhibits comparable leaching efficiency to transition metals, highlighting that stronger solvation may enhance its mobility and availability for sulfate complexation under acidic conditions.

This study discussed the LIBs recycling using a bio-reductant, which provided valuable insights into the dissolution of Li, Ni, Co, and Mn from BM. Bell-pepper extract (BPE) turned an agri-food residue into a high-strength bio-reductant that together with sulfuric acid outperformed conventional H₂SO₄/H₂O₂ leaching chemistry. The presence of vital compounds such as ascorbic acid and phenolic compounds, its exceptional antioxidant load (307 mg Trolox L⁻¹) and phenolic content (229 mg gallic-acid L⁻¹) was enough to drive near-quantitative dissolution of Li, Ni, Mn, and Co from NMC black mass. Under the optimized hydrometallurgical conditions (30 vol% BPE, 2 M H2SO4, 70 °C, 20 g/L pulp, 120 min) metal dissolutions exceed 96 % and the post-leach residue is essentially metal-free, surpassing conventional routes without the need for synthetic reductant. Even when the pulp density was quadrupled to 100 g/L, BPE retained high effectiveness; a modest temperature increase to 90 °C restored full dissolution, confirming the extract’s robustness under industrial throughputs.

The analysis of surface charge density and radial distribution functions (RDF) reveals that strong electrostatic interactions between free cations (Co2+, Li+, Mn2+, and Ni2+) and molecular components in 2.0 M H2SO4 are critical for complex formation, with significant attractive forces observed between these cations and sulfate ions at specific distances. By coupling waste valorization with high-yield leaching efficiency, this study contributes to the development of more effective and environmentally friendly processes for the extraction of valuable metals from secondary resources, supporting circular-economy recycling of lithium-ion batteries.
